projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

observations.summary: The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds consistent with an alarm or sharp transient, but the context suggests it might be related to the dogs or general outdoor noise. — The high spectral centroid and high high-band energy suggest a sharp, potentially electronic or percussive sound, but the low overall energy and the presence of dogs make a definitive classification difficult. The rule guess leans towards 'alarm' but the overall profile is ambiguous.

The audio contains distinct bursts of sound consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy and the peak/RMS ratio suggest transient, vocal sounds, fitting the profile of barks, which is consistent with the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs.

The camera stream experienced a technical error, and the visible frames show an empty dog run. However, audio indicates that dogs are barking in the area, suggesting activity that is not visually captured.

The audio contains distinct, intermittent sounds consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy and the peak/RMS ratio suggest sharp, transient sounds typical of barks, which aligns with the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs.

The audio contains distinct sounds consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy and the rule guess strongly suggest barking, which is expected given the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds that are not clearly identifiable as a specific event like a bark or alarm in the front yard. — The high spectral centroid and high high-band energy suggest a sharp, possibly electronic or impact-related sound, but the overall pattern is too ambiguous for a confident classification.

The front door camera is non-functional and is not receiving any video frames, displaying only an error message. While audio indicates that the dogs are barking in the dog run, there is no visual information available from this camera.

The audio contains distinct, intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dogs barking in the dog run area. — The high peak/RMS ratio (8.17) and the energy distribution across mid-band frequencies strongly suggest vocalizations like barks, which aligns with the presence of two dogs in the dog run.

The audio contains distinct, sharp bursts of sound consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy and the peak/RMS ratio suggest transient, vocal sounds, fitting the profile of barks, which is consistent with the presence of two dogs.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds consistent with an alarm or sharp transient, recorded in the front yard. — The high spectral centroid (3466 Hz) and high high-band energy (0.648) suggest a sharp, possibly electronic sound, but the low overall energy and low zero-crossing rate make definitive classification difficult. The rule guess points to 'alarm' with moderate confidence.

The audio contains intermittent, sharp sounds consistent with activity in the dog run area. — The high peak/RMS ratio (4.50) suggests transients, and the energy distribution across mid and high bands could indicate barking or other outdoor activity, but the rule guess for 'alarm' is not strongly supported by the context or other features.
An unknown person walks past the driveway where an unknown grey SUV is parked. The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds that may be dogs barking in the background.

The audio appears to contain intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy (0.439) and the visual transients in the spectrogram, combined with the context of two dogs, strongly suggest barking, despite the rule guess favoring 'alarm'.

The audio contains intermittent, broadband energy bursts consistent with some outdoor activity, but no clear signature matches the known categories. — The high mid-band and high-band energy suggest potential transient sounds, but the low overall RMS level and low zero-crossing rate make definitive classification difficult.
Music in kitchen: JAŸ-Z, Amil & Ja Rule - Can I Get A... [feat. Amil & Ja Rule] [Explicit]
LEAK DETECTED. Flow: 0.26 gal/min. Daily total: 125.4 gal.

The audio appears to contain intermittent, low-energy sounds consistent with general background noise in the living room. — The spectral centroid and mid-band energy suggest some vocal or percussive elements, but the overall low energy and low zero-crossing rate make definitive classification difficult. The rule guess of 'impact' is plausible but not certain.

The audio appears to contain low-level, intermittent background noise consistent with an outdoor setting, but no distinct sounds like barking or alarms were clearly detected. — The spectral centroid and mid-band energy suggest some activity, but the low energy variance and low overall RMS level make definitive classification difficult.

The audio contains intermittent, moderate-frequency sounds consistent with general outdoor activity, but no distinct event is clearly identifiable. — The spectral centroid and mid-band energy suggest some vocalization or transient sounds, but the low energy and low zero-crossing rate make definitive classification difficult. The rule guess of 'alarm' seems overly confident given the overall low energy.

The audio contains distinct, intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dog barking, likely originating from the entryway area. — The high Mid-band energy (0.446) and the rule guess pointing to 'bark' are supported by the visual evidence of sharp, vertical energy spikes in the spectrogram, which is typical for barks. The high Peak/RMS ratio (10.52) also supports transient sounds like barks.

insights.summary: [dog_run] Technical error in camera stream; audio indicates dog activity despite empty visual frames.
[doorbell] The front door camera is currently non-functional and is not receiving any video frames.
[front_yard] An unknown person and an unrecognized vehicle are present in the driveway.
[backyard] Audio indicates dog barking and an alarm at the front yard, but no activity is visible in the backyard.
[front_yard] An unknown person is walking near the property while an unrecognized vehicle is parked in the driveway.
[dog_run] Camera stream error detected; audio indicates barking in the dog run while no dogs are visible.
[doorbell] The front door camera is non-functional and is not receiving any video frames.
[backyard] Audio suggests potential alarm or barking, but no activity is visible in the camera frame.
[front_yard] An unknown person is walking near the gate and an unknown grey SUV is parked in the driveway.
[doorbell] The front door camera is not providing a video feed, preventing visual confirmation of the activity suggested by the audio.
